instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Healthy Cooking Oils for Diabetics: What to Choose
Diabetes is becoming a major concern, and every time you switch on the TV, it’s flooded with ads claiming their cooking oil is the “best for your heart.” It’s no surprise you end up confused, wondering which bottle to trust and what actually works to keep your blood sugar in check.
When you have diabetes, the oil you cook matters more than you think. It’s not about taste alone, but about your heart health, weight, and long-term glucose control. Well, the good news is that you don’t need expensive or fancy options to achieve this goal. Let us know about some healthy cooking oil options that can make the meal tasty and nutritious.
Understanding MUFA and PUFA
Fat is energy-dense; just one teaspoon of oil carries about 45 calories.1 So, the first rule is simple: always use oil in a controlled manner. Too much oil can contribute to weight gain and insulin resistance, which isn’t ideal when you’re managing diabetes. Still, completely avoiding fat isn’t the goal either. Your body needs some fat to support the nervous system and to help absorb vitamins.1
When it comes to choosing healthier oils, not all fats behave the same way.
Monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFA) are your best bet. Oils like groundnut, mustard, and sesame fall into this group. They help raise HDL (the “good” cholesterol) while lowering LDL (the “bad” cholesterol), making them friendly for both heart and blood sugar health.
Polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) comes next. Sunflower and safflower oils are common examples. They don’t raise LDL, but some can lower HDL slightly.1
Other Common Fats You Should Know
There are a few more types of oils present in your kitchen that react differently on your body.
Some, like palm oil, coconut oil, and even ghee, firm up at room temperature because they’re high in saturated fat. These can increase your LDL levels, so it’s better to consume them in small amounts.
Next, trans fats, often found in vanaspati, are best to be avoided altogether. Hydrogenated oils fall in the same bucket as they’re added to packaged foods to increase shelf life, but can seriously raise your risk of weight gain and metabolic issues.
Refined oils aren’t great either. Most of their nutrients get removed during processing, and the high-heat methods can trigger the formation of harmful trans fats.
If you want a cleaner option, go for cold-pressed oils. They skip the heat, retain more natural goodness, and are generally a safer everyday choice.1
Which Healthy Cooking Oils Should Diabetics Use?
If you have diabetes, you can use any of the following oils in everyday cooking for a healthy heart and steady sugar.
Smart Way to Rotate Your Cooking Oils
Switching your oils from time to time is a simple habit that actually works in your favour. Even if you're using mostly unsaturated oils, your body benefits when you don’t stick to just one type for months.1 For example, if you’ve been using rice bran oil for everyday cooking, replace it with corn oil for some time, and later bring in olive oil or another healthy option.
This little rotation trick helps you avoid over-relying on a single fatty acid profile. Each oil brings its own mix of MUFA and PUFA, and when you keep alternating them, you naturally end up with a more balanced spread of healthy fats without any complicated diet rules.1
Choose Healthy Oil for a Fitter Version
Healthy cooking oils aren’t about the latest trends or fancy labels but about giving your body the support it needs, especially if you’re diabetic. By picking oils rich in MUFA and PUFA, using them moderately and switching regularly, you’re already taking a big step toward steadier blood sugar and better cardiac health.
However, oil is just one part of the bigger picture. A balanced diet with enough protein and fibre, light exercise, and securing your family’s future are essential too. A diabetic-friendly term plan like Bajaj Life Diabetic Term Plan II Sub 8 HbA1c adds an extra layer of protection so you can focus on your well-being with peace of mind.
Key Takeaways
The type of oil and how much you use matter just as much as the cooking method.
MUFA-rich oils like groundnut, mustard, and olive oil are generally the most diabetes-friendly.
PUFA oils like sunflower oil work well too, but it’s healthier to rotate them instead of sticking to one.
Oils high in saturated or trans fats should be limited to protect your cholesterol balance.
Options like rice bran, avocado, olive, peanut, canola, sesame, and flaxseed oil each offer their own benefits.
Switching between oils gives your body a better mix of healthy fats and supports steady blood sugar.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
Which cooking oil is considered best for diabetics?
Rice bran oil is often recommended because it’s rich in MUFA and contains oryzanol, a compound that helps lower cholesterol and calm inflammation. 1
Can saturated fats like coconut oil be used by diabetics?
Yes, but in moderation. Coconut oil contains saturated fats, so it shouldn’t be your main cooking oil. Since it supports weight control and raises good cholesterol, you can use it occasionally in small amounts.
Why is cooking oil at high heat not good for you?
When oil is overheated or reaches its smoke point, it breaks down, loses nutrients, and can form harmful compounds. Deep-frying or repeatedly reheating oil changes its fatty acids, increases carcinogenic byproducts, and makes food absorb more oil, raising long-term risks for your blood vessels and cardiovascular system.1
Should diabetics stick to one oil or keep switching?
Each oil type offers a different mix of healthy fats and antioxidants. Switching between options like rice bran, olive, groundnut, and canola helps you get a more balanced nutrient profile over time.
Why do many Indians prefer mustard oil for cooking?Mustard oil is popular because it supports better heart wellness. Its natural cold-pressed form has a balanced Omega-6 to Omega-3 ratio and is rich in MUFA and PUFA, while being low in saturated fat, making it a healthier choice for everyday cooking.4
Disclaimer

It is provided solely for your reference purpose and not to be construed as investment advice. output.final_text:
Glycaemic Index: Foods That Support Balanced Sugar Levels
When it comes to managing diabetes, the right nutrition plays a pivotal role. What you eat has a direct impact on your blood sugar levels, which is why medical professionals stress the importance of eating right.
For diabetics, eating right often involves choosing food which is low in sugar and carbohydrate (carb) content. Besides sugar and carbs, one more factor to choose the right food item is the glycaemic index. The glycaemic index of food items is also considered when choosing whether to include them in a diabetic’s diet plan or not.
Let’s understand what the glycaemic index is, why it's important and the right food items that can balance blood sugar levels.
What is the glycaemic index?
In simple terms, glycaemic index (GI) is a type of ranking system which is used to assess food items that contain carbohydrates. The glycaemic index rates food items based on the time taken to affect blood glucose levels. Let’s simplify. 1
Food items that contain carbs are broken down into glucose when consumed. This glucose impacts your blood sugar levels.1 If the glucose gets quickly absorbed and spikes blood sugar, the food is said to have a high glycaemic index. On the other hand, if the time taken for glucose absorption is low and the blood sugar doesn’t spike quickly, the glycaemic index is low. 2
Categorisation of glycaemic index
The glycaemic index ranges from 0 to 100. Here’s how it’s categorised3 –
Range Categorisation Meaning 4
0 to 55 Low Food items with a low GI do not increase blood sugar quickly. They are good for diabetics
56 to 69 Moderate Food items with a moderate GI can be included in a diabetic’s diet, but less frequently 70-100 High Food items with high GI raise blood sugar quickly and are recommended to be avoided by diabetics.
Why is the glycaemic index an important factor for diabetes management?
The glycaemic index is a useful measure for diabetics. Here are some reasons why it is important –
1. Knowing the glycaemic index of different food items makes it easy to choose the right ones. 2. You can plan a balanced meal combining sources of protein, fats, and low-GI carbs. 3. Choosing low GI food can help in maintaining blood sugar levels steady. 4. When the blood sugar doesn’t spike immediately after meals, insulin can work more
effectively. 5. Food items with a low GI have a high fibre content, which improves satiety, helps you avoid
overeating, and can help in weight management.
Factors affecting the glycaemic index of food items
The glycaemic index of food items can change depending on various factors such as3 –
1. Cooking process:
Frying or roasting food items tends to increase the GI, while boiling reduces it. 2. Processing of raw food:
Processed food items have a higher GI than unprocessed food. For instance, polished grains, white bread, refined flour, etc., have a high GI, while unpolished grains, whole-wheat flour, and brown bread have a low GI4. 3. Ripeness of fruits:
The GI of fruits increases as they ripen. 4. Food combinations:
Pairing carb-containing food items with lean proteins, fats, etc., helps in lowering the overall GI. 5. Fibre content:
Food items having a high fibre content have a lower GI compared to food items with less or no fibre.
Glycaemic index of common Indian food items
Now that you know what glycaemic index is and how it is important, the next thing to know is the GI of common Indian food items so that you can choose right. As such, the following table mentions the approximate GI values of common food items, rating them in the high, moderate and low GI categories –
GI category4 Food categories4 Common examples4 Low (0 to 55) Vegetables ● Lady’s finger
● Cabbage
● Onion
● Brinjal
● Green peas
● Cucumber
● Carrot
● Broccoli
● Tomato
● Leafy vegetables, etc.
Fruits ● Apples
● Dates
● Oranges
● Pears
● Kiwi
● Berries
● Banana
● Mango
● Pomegranate, etc.
Whole grains and cereals ● Quinoa
● Barley
● Oats
● Corn
● Poha
● Daliya
● Whole wheat, etc.
Pulses ● Soyabean
● Lobia
● Chola
● Kidney beans
● Green gram
● Lentils, etc.
Dairy products ● Buttermilk
● Milk
● Yoghurt
● Paneer
● Cheese, etc.
Other items ● Nuts
● Fish
● Pumpkin seeds
● Eggs
● Meat
● Chicken
● Dark and milk chocolate, etc.
Moderate (56 to 69) Vegetables ● Beetroot
● Yam
● Sweet potato, etc.
Fruits ● Pineapple
● Papaya
● Raisins, etc.
Whole grains and cereals ● Brown and basmati rice
● Muesli
● Rye, etc.
Dairy products ● Ice cream, etc.
Other items ● Table and brown sugar
● Honey
● Soft drinks
● Noodles, etc.
High (70 to 100) Vegetables ● Pumpkin
● White potato, etc.
Fruits ● Muskmelon
● Watermelon
● Grapes, etc.
Whole grains and cereals ● White rice
● Jowar
● Wheat
● Instant or ready-to-eat oats
● Maida, etc.
Other items ● Jaggery
● Cookies
● Cakes
● Cornflakes, etc.
Choosing the right approach for effective diabetes management
When considering food items as a diabetic, it is important to assess not only the food’s sugar and carb content, but its glycaemic index too. Choosing food items with a low glycaemic index can help in maintaining blood glucose levels and promote insulin sensitivity.
So, consult with a medical professional and create a balanced diet combining proteins, fats, and carbs with a low GI so that you consume the right nutrients without affecting your diabetes.
Key takeaways
1. The glycaemic index is a tool to measure the time taken by carbohydrate-containing food
items to affect blood sugar levels. 2. Glycaemic index ranges from 0 to 100 and has been categorised in three levels – low (0-55),
moderate (56-69), and high (70 – 100). 3. Food items that have a high glycaemic index tend to spike blood sugar quickly and are not
suitable for diabetics. 4. The glycaemic index is affected by cooking, ripeness of fruits, food pairings, processing of
food items, etc. 5. Common examples of low GI food items include whole grains, non-starchy vegetables, low-
fat dairy, etc. 6. Food with high GI includes processed foods, sweet potato, sugary and starchy food items,
etc.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
1. How to include food with low GI in the diet?
Some common ways to include low-GI food items in your diet include choosing whole grains and high-fibre food items, avoiding packaged food, and including a lot of vegetables and fruits with a low GI rating. 2. Are all low-GI food items healthy?
While most low-GI food items are healthy, some exceptions might be there. For instance, dark and milk chocolate have a low GI, but they have high sugar content and unnecessary fats that can be avoided3. 3. What is glycaemic load?
Glycaemic load is a more holistic approach to choosing food as it combines the glycaemic index of a food item with the amount of carb it contains. The glycaemic load measures the GI and carb content of the portion size that you consume. 1
For instance, watermelon has a high GI but very low carb content. So, if you eat a moderate quantity of watermelon, your blood sugar would not rise very quickly because of the low carb content.5 Similarly, pasta has a low GI but high carb content. So, if you eat a large portion of it, your blood sugar might rise quickly despite the low GI.1 4. Should I consider only the GI when choosing food items?
Besides GI, it is better to choose food items based on their glycaemic load, protein and fibre content, calories, etc. 5. How to lower the glycaemic effect in my meals?
You can lower the effect of GI in your meals by combining proteins and fibre with carbs3. This creates a more balanced approach to nutrition and also reduces the overall GI of your meal.
Disclaimer

Kindly consult your Dietician/Nutritionist/Doctor for any assistance/advice.
